content.text: Food is as essential for living as air is for breathing. Food security means something more than getting two square meals. It has three interconnected dimensions: availability, accessibility, and affordability.

content.definition: Food security means availability, accessibility and affordability of food to all people at all times.

content.title: Dimensions of Food Security
items: Availability: Food production within the country, food imports, and previous years' stock stored in government granaries.
Accessibility: Food is within reach of every person, with no barriers to access.
Affordability: An individual has enough money to buy sufficient, safe, and nutritious food to meet dietary needs.

content.title: Conditions for Food Security
content.text: Food security is ensured in a country only if: (1) enough food is available for all persons, (2) all persons have the capacity to buy food of acceptable quality, and (3) there is no barrier on access to food.

In the 1970s, food security was understood as the 'availability at all times of adequate supply of basic foodstuffs' (UN, 1975). Amartya Sen added a new dimension to food security and emphasised the 'access' to food through what he called 'entitlements'—a combination of what one can produce, exchange in the market along with state or other socially provided supplies.

The 1995 World Food Summit declared, 'Food security at the individual, household, regional, national and global levels exists when all people, at all times, have physical and economic access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food to meet their dietary needs and food preferences for an active and healthy life' (FAO, 1996, p.3). The declaration further recognises that 'poverty eradication is essential to improve access to food'.

The poorest section of society might be food insecure most of the time, while persons above the poverty line might also be food insecure when the country faces a national disaster or calamity like earthquake, drought, flood, tsunami, widespread failure of crops causing famine, etc.

During a natural calamity like drought, total production of foodgrains decreases, creating a shortage of food in affected areas. Due to shortage, prices go up, and some people cannot afford to buy food. If such a calamity happens in a very widespread area or is stretched over a longer time period, it may cause a situation of starvation. A massive starvation might take the turn of a famine.

content.definition: A famine is characterised by widespread deaths due to starvation and epidemics caused by forced use of contaminated water or decaying food and loss of body resistance due to weakening from starvation.

content.text: The most devastating famine that occurred in India was the Famine of Bengal in 1943. This famine killed thirty lakh people in the province of Bengal. The agricultural labourers, fishermen, transport workers, and other casual labourers were affected the most by dramatically increasing price of rice. They were the ones who died in this famine.

content.title: Table 4.1: Production of Rice in the Province of Bengal
Key Observation
content.text: Nothing like the Bengal famine has happened in India again. However, even today, famine-like conditions exist in many parts of the country, leading to starvation deaths at times. Natural calamities and pandemics may also lead to food shortage. For example, the Covid-19 pandemic had an adverse impact upon food security due to restrictions on movement of people, goods, and services impacting economic activity.

Although a large section of people suffer from food and nutrition insecurity in India, the worst affected groups are: landless people with little or no land to depend upon, traditional artisans, providers of traditional services, petty self-employed workers, and destitutes including beggars.

In urban areas, the food insecure families are those whose working members are generally employed in ill-paid occupations and the casual labour market. These workers are largely engaged in seasonal activities and are paid very low wages that just ensure bare survival.

The social composition along with the inability to buy food also plays a role in food insecurity. The SCs, STs, and some sections of the OBCs (lower castes among them) who have either poor land-base or very low land productivity are prone to food insecurity. People affected by natural disasters, who have to migrate to other areas in search of work, are also among the most food insecure people.

A high incidence of malnutrition prevails among women. This puts even the unborn baby at the risk of malnutrition. A large proportion of pregnant and nursing mothers and children under the age of 5 years constitute an important segment of the food insecure population. According to the National Health and Family Survey (NHFS) 1998–99, the number of such women and children is approximately 11 crore.

The food insecure people are disproportionately large in some regions of the country, such as economically backward states with high incidence of poverty, tribal and remote areas, regions more prone to natural disasters, etc. The states of Uttar Pradesh (eastern and south-eastern parts), Bihar, Jharkhand, Orissa, West Bengal, Chhattisgarh, parts of Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra account for the largest number of food insecure people in the country.

Ramu works as a casual labourer in agriculture in Raipur village. Agriculture being a seasonal activity employs Ramu only during times of sowing, transplanting, and harvesting. He remains unemployed for about 4 months during the period of plant consolidation and maturing in a year. He looks for work in other activities like brick laying or construction. By all his efforts, Ramu is able to earn enough for two square meals for his family. However, during days when he is unable to get work, he and his family face difficulties, and sometimes his small kids have to sleep without food. Ramu is food insecure during the 4 months when he remains unemployed because of the seasonal nature of agriculture work.

Ahmad is a rickshaw puller in Bangalore. He stays in a jhuggi with his 3 brothers, 2 sisters, and old parents. His earnings fluctuate every day. On some days he earns enough to save, while on other days he barely earns enough to buy daily necessities. Fortunately, Ahmad has a yellow card (PDS card for below poverty line people). With this card, Ahmad gets sufficient quantity of wheat, rice, sugar, and kerosene oil at half the market price. He purchases his monthly stock from the ration shop, enabling him to eke out survival for his big family where he is the only earning member.

Hunger is another aspect indicating food insecurity. Hunger is not just an expression of poverty; it brings about poverty. The attainment of food security therefore involves eliminating current hunger and reducing the risks of future hunger.

content.definition: A consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity and/or quality. Poor people suffer from chronic hunger because of their very low income and inability to buy food even for survival.

Related to cycles of food growing and harvesting. Prevalent in rural areas because of the seasonal nature of agricultural activities and in urban areas because of casual labourers (e.g., less work for construction labourers during rainy season).

content.title: Table 4.2: Percentage of Households with 'Hunger' in India
Declining Trend
content.text: The percentage of seasonal, as well as chronic hunger has declined in India, as shown in the table above.

India has been aiming at self-sufficiency in foodgrains since Independence. After Independence, Indian policymakers adopted all measures to achieve self-sufficiency in food grains. India adopted a new strategy in agriculture, which resulted in the 'Green Revolution', especially in the production of wheat and rice.

Indira Gandhi, the then Prime Minister of India, officially recorded the impressive strides of Green Revolution in agriculture by releasing a special stamp entitled 'Wheat Revolution' in July 1968. The success of wheat was later replicated in rice.

Since the advent of the Green Revolution in the early 1970s, the country has avoided famine even during adverse weather conditions. India has become self-sufficient in foodgrains during the last 30 years because of a variety of crops grown all over the country.

The increase in foodgrains was, however, disproportionate. The highest rate of growth was achieved in Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh. The total foodgrain production was 310 million tonnes in 2020–21 and increased to 315 million tonnes in 2021–22.

Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh recorded significant production in wheat—36 and 18 million tonnes in 2020–21 respectively. West Bengal and UP recorded significant production of rice—17 and 16 million tonnes in 2020–21 respectively.

content.title: Graph 4.1: Production of Foodgrains in India (Million Tonnes)
content.description: The graph shows foodgrain production from 1960-61 to 2021-22, demonstrating a steady increase from 80 million tonnes in 1960-61 to 328 million tonnes in 2021-22.

topics.topic_name: Two Components of Food Security System

content.text: The availability of foodgrains at the country level has further been ensured with a carefully designed food security system by the government. This system has two components: (a) buffer stock, and (b) public distribution system.

content.definition: The stock of foodgrains, namely wheat and rice, procured by the government through the Food Corporation of India (FCI).

content.text: The FCI purchases wheat and rice from farmers in states where there is surplus production. Farmers are paid a pre-announced price for their crops called the Minimum Support Price (MSP). The MSP is declared by the government every year before the sowing season to provide incentives to farmers for raising the production of these crops.

content.definition: The pre-announced price at which the government purchases foodgrains from farmers to provide incentives for raising production.

content.text: The purchased foodgrains are stored in granaries. Buffer stock is created to distribute foodgrains in deficit areas and among the poorer strata of society at a price lower than the market price (Issue Price). This also helps resolve the problem of shortage of food during adverse weather conditions or periods of calamity.

content.definition: The system through which food procured by FCI is distributed through government-regulated ration shops among the poorer sections of society.

content.text: Ration shops, also known as Fair Price Shops, are present in most localities, villages, towns, and cities—about 5.5 lakh ration shops all over the country. They keep stock of foodgrains, sugar, and kerosene for cooking, sold to people at a price lower than the market price.

content.title: Types of Ration Cards
items: Antyodaya cards: For the poorest of the poor
BPL cards: For those below poverty line
APL cards: For all others (above poverty line)

content.text: Any family with a ration card can buy a stipulated amount of these items (e.g., 35 kg of grains, 5 litres of kerosene, 5 kgs of sugar, etc.) every month from the nearby ration shop.

The introduction of rationing in India dates back to the 1940s against the backdrop of the Bengal famine. The rationing system was revived in the wake of acute food shortage during the 1960s, prior to the Green Revolution.

In the wake of high incidence of poverty levels as reported by the NSSO in the mid-1970s, three important food intervention programmes were introduced: Public Distribution System (PDS) for food grains (strengthened thereafter); Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) (introduced in 1975 on an experimental basis); and Food-for-Work (FFW) (introduced in 1977–78).

In the beginning, the coverage of PDS was universal with no discrimination between the poor and the non-poor. Over the years, the policy related to PDS has been revised to make it more efficient and targeted.

content.title: Key Milestones in PDS Evolution
items: 1992: Revamped Public Distribution System (RPDS) introduced in 1,700 blocks to provide benefits of PDS to remote and backward areas.
1997: Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS) introduced to adopt the principle of targeting the 'poor in all areas'. Differential price policy was adopted for poor and non-poor.
2000: Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) launched for the 'poorest of the poor'—25 kg of foodgrains at highly subsidised rates (`2/kg for wheat, `3/kg for rice).
2000: Annapurna Scheme (APS) launched for indigent senior citizens.
2002: Quantity under AAY enhanced from 25 to 35 kg.
2003-2004: AAY expanded twice by additional 50 lakh BPL families, covering 2 crore families.

content.text: This Act provides for food and nutritional security at affordable prices and enables people to live a life with dignity. Under this Act, 75% of the rural population and 50% of the urban population have been categorised as eligible households for food security.

content.title: Table 4.3: Some Important Features of PDS

content.text: The PDS has proved to be the most effective instrument of government policy over the years in stabilising prices and making food available to consumers at affordable prices. It has been instrumental in averting widespread hunger and famine by supplying food from surplus regions to deficit ones. The system, including MSP and procurement, has contributed to an increase in foodgrain production and provided income security to farmers in certain regions.

However, FCI godowns are overflowing with grains, with some rotting away and some being eaten by rats. In 2022, the stock of wheat and rice with Central pool was 159 LMT of wheat and 104 LMT of rice—much more than the minimum buffer norms. These remained consistently higher than buffer norms.

content.title: Graph 4.2: Central Foodgrains (Wheat + Rice) Stock and Minimum Buffer Norm (Million Tonnes)
content.description: The graph shows foodgrain stocks consistently exceeding buffer norms over the years.

content.text: There is a general consensus that high level of buffer stocks is very undesirable and can be wasteful. The storage of massive food stocks has been responsible for high carrying costs, in addition to wastage and deterioration in grain quality. Freezing of MSP for a few years should be considered seriously.

The increased foodgrains procurement at enhanced MSP is the result of pressure exerted by leading foodgrain producing states, such as Punjab, Haryana, and Andhra Pradesh. As procurement is concentrated in a few prosperous regions and mainly of two crops—wheat and rice—increase in MSP has induced farmers to divert land from production of coarse grains (staple food of the poor) to the production of rice and wheat.

The intensive utilisation of water in the cultivation of rice has also led to environmental degradation and fall in water level, threatening the sustainability of agricultural development in these states. The rising MSP has raised the maintenance cost of procuring foodgrains. Rising transportation and storage costs of the FCI are other contributing factors.

PDS dealers are sometimes found resorting to malpractices like diverting grains to the open market to get better margin, selling poor quality grains at ration shops, and irregular opening of shops. Ration shops regularly have unsold stocks of poor quality grains left, leading to piling up of massive stocks with FCI.

In recent years, another factor has led to the decline of PDS. Earlier, every family (poor and non-poor) had a ration card with fixed quota of items at the same low price. Now, with TPDS of three different prices, any family above poverty line gets very little discount—the price for APL family is almost as high as open market price, so there is little incentive for them to buy from ration shops.

As per the NSSO report No. 558, in rural India, per person per month consumption of rice declined from 6.38 kg in 2004–05 to 5.98 kg in 2011–12. In urban India, it declined from 4.71 kg to 4.49 kg. However, per capita consumption of PDS rice doubled in rural India and increased by 66% in urban India since 2004–05. Per capita consumption of PDS wheat doubled in both rural and urban India since 2004–05.

notes.section_title: Role of Cooperatives in Food Security

content.text: Cooperatives are playing an important role in food security in India, especially in the southern and western parts of the country. Cooperative societies set up shops to sell low-priced goods to poor people.

content.title: Examples of Cooperatives
items: Tamil Nadu: Around 94% of fair price shops are run by cooperatives.
Delhi: Mother Dairy provides milk and vegetables at controlled rates.
Gujarat: Amul (milk and milk products) brought about the White Revolution.

content.text: In Maharashtra, the Academy of Development Science (ADS) has facilitated a network of NGOs for setting up grain banks in different regions. ADS organises training and capacity building programmes on food security for NGOs. Grain Banks are now slowly taking shape in different parts of Maharashtra. The ADS Grain Bank programme is acknowledged as a successful and innovative food security intervention.

glossary.definition: Availability, accessibility and affordability of food to all people at all times.
Food production within the country, food imports, and previous years' stock stored in government granaries.
Food being within reach of every person with no barriers to access.
An individual having enough money to buy sufficient, safe and nutritious food to meet dietary needs.
Widespread deaths due to starvation and epidemics caused by forced use of contaminated water or decaying food and loss of body resistance due to weakening from starvation.
Consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity and/or quality; poor people suffer from this due to very low income.
Hunger related to cycles of food growing and harvesting; prevalent in rural areas due to seasonal nature of agriculture and in urban areas due to casual labour.
Stock of foodgrains (wheat and rice) procured by the government through FCI to distribute in deficit areas and among poorer sections at prices lower than market price.
Pre-announced price at which the government purchases foodgrains from farmers to provide incentives for raising production.
Price lower than market price at which foodgrains from buffer stock are distributed to the poorer sections of society.
System through which food procured by FCI is distributed through government-regulated ration shops among the poorer sections of society.
Shops that keep stock of foodgrains, sugar, and kerosene, selling them to people at prices lower than market price.
Scheme launched in 2000 providing 35 kg of foodgrains per month to poorest BPL families at highly subsidised rates (`2/kg for wheat, `3/kg for rice).
System introduced in 1997 adopting differential price policy for poor and non-poor, targeting the 'poor in all areas'.
Agricultural strategy adopted in India that resulted in significant increase in production of wheat and rice, making India self-sufficient in foodgrains.
Payment a government makes to a producer to supplement the market price of a commodity; helps keep consumer prices low while maintaining higher income for domestic producers.
Government agency responsible for procurement and storage of foodgrains as buffer stock.

summary_points: Food security has three dimensions: availability (production, imports, stocks), accessibility (food within reach), and affordability (ability to buy).
Food insecurity affects landless labourers, traditional artisans, casual workers, SCs, STs, and OBCs with poor land-base; women and children under 5 are particularly vulnerable.
The Bengal Famine of 1943 killed thirty lakh people; India has avoided famine since the Green Revolution, though famine-like conditions persist in some areas.
Hunger has two dimensions: chronic (persistent inadequate diet) and seasonal (related to agricultural cycles); both have declined significantly since 1983.
India's food security system has two components: Buffer Stock (procured by FCI at MSP) and Public Distribution System (distribution through ration shops).
PDS evolved from universal coverage (pre-1992) to targeted system: RPDS (1992), TPDS (1997), AAY and APS (2000), and NFSA (2013).
Buffer stock often exceeds norms, leading to high carrying costs, wastage, and environmental concerns due to over-concentration on wheat and rice in a few states.
PDS faces challenges: dealer malpractices, poor quality grains, and declining APL participation due to prices nearly matching market rates.
Cooperatives (e.g., Amul, Mother Dairy) and NGOs (e.g., ADS Grain Banks) play significant roles in supplementing government food security efforts.
The National Food Security Act, 2013 provides legal entitlement to food for 75% of rural and 50% of urban population.
